scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CITY CIVIL JUDGE, BENGALURU

ORIGINAL SUIT NO. _____ OF 2024

Anita Sharma,
D/o Shri Ramesh Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
R/o No. 45, 12th Main, Indiranagar,
Bengaluru, Karnataka – 560038.                                                    ... PLAINTIFF

VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
S/o Shri Krishna Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
R/o No. 78, Sector 2, HSR Layout,
Bengaluru, Karnataka – 560102.                                                     ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF AGREEMENT TO SELL

The above-named Plaintiff most respectfully submits as follows:-

I. PRELIMINARY SUBMISSIONS

1. This suit is filed for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024 executed between the Plaintiff and the Defendant in respect of the scheduled property, namely, Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft, for a total sale consideration of Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty-Five Lakhs only).

2. The subject matter of the suit is immovable property situated within the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court. The Defendant resides and the scheduled property is situated within the local limits of this Hon'ble Court. Hence, this Hon'ble Court has jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit.

3. The Plaintiff has paid the requisite Court Fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} on the suit value of Rs. 85,00,000/- as per the Karnataka Court-Fees and Suits Valuation Act, 1958.

4. There is no other suit pending, filed or decided, on the same subject matter in this Hon'ble Court or any other Court in India. There is no other parallel proceeding between the same parties on the same subject matter.

5. The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court. The parties reside within Bengaluru and the scheduled property is situated within Bengaluru.

II. FACTS OF THE CASE

6. The Plaintiff is a bona fide purchaser intending to purchase a residential property for her personal residence and investment purposes. The Defendant is the absolute owner and is entitled to sell and convey the scheduled property.

7. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into a written Agreement to Sell for the purchase of Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft, for a total sale consideration of Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty-Five Lakhs only). The true copy of the Agreement to Sell is marked as ANNEXURE-A.

8. On the same day, i.e., 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff paid an advance/earnest money of Rs. 25,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Lakhs only) to the Defendant through RTGS mode, the transaction reference number being HDFC20240110987654. The Defendant acknowledged receipt of the said amount. The RTGS receipt is marked as ANNEXURE-B.

9. As per the terms of the Agreement to Sell, the balance sale consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- (Rupees Sixty Lakhs only) was to be paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on or before 10.07.2024, upon execution and registration of the Sale Deed in favour of the Plaintiff.

10. The Agreement to Sell contains an express covenant that the Defendant shall execute the Sale Deed and convey absolute title over the scheduled property to the Plaintiff upon receipt of the balance consideration.

III. READINESS AND WILLINGNESS

11. The Plaintiff has at all times been and continues to be ready and willing to perform her part of the contract. The Plaintiff has arranged the balance sale consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- and has been ready to pay the same upon the Defendant executing the Sale Deed. The Plaintiff's readiness and willingness is evident from the fact that she had made arrangements for the balance amount well before the stipulated date of 10.07.2024.

12. The Plaintiff has complied with all her obligations under the Agreement to Sell and has been ready and willing to complete the transaction. The Plaintiff's conduct throughout has demonstrated her bona fides and intention to purchase the scheduled property.

13. Section 16(c) of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 requires that the Plaintiff must have performed or have been ready and willing to perform the essential terms of the contract. The Plaintiff satisfies this requirement as she has paid the earnest money and has always been ready and willing to pay the balance consideration and complete the transaction.

IV. DEFENDANT'S BREACH AND REFUSAL

14. On 05.06.2024, the Defendant sent a letter to the Plaintiff refusing to execute the Sale Deed, stating that he had received a higher offer for the scheduled property from another party. The said letter constitutes a clear breach of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024. The Defendant's refusal letter is marked as ANNEXURE-C.

15. The Defendant's refusal to execute the Sale Deed on the ground of receiving a higher offer is unlawful and contrary to the terms of the Agreement to Sell. The Defendant is bound by the agreement and cannot resile from his contractual obligations on the pretext of a higher offer.

16. The Defendant's conduct amounts to breach of contract and the Plaintiff is entitled to specific performance of the Agreement to Sell as a matter of right under Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963.

V. LEGAL NOTICE

17. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to execute the Sale Deed in terms of the Agreement to Sell. The legal notice was sent by registered post and was duly served on the Defendant. The legal notice along with the postal receipt is marked as ANNEXURE-D.

18. The Defendant refused to comply with the legal notice and did not execute the Sale Deed. The Defendant continues to refuse to perform his contractual obligations despite the Plaintiff's readiness and willingness.

VI. LEGAL BASIS

19. Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 provides that specific performance of a contract shall be granted by the Court, except in cases where the contract is of such a nature that compensation in money would afford adequate relief. The present contract for sale of immovable property is specifically enforceable as the scheduled property is not readily available in the market and compensation in money would not afford adequate relief to the Plaintiff.

20. Section 16 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 prescribes the circumstances under which specific performance cannot be granted. The Plaintiff has not failed to perform any essential term of the contract and has always been ready and willing to perform her obligations. Therefore, the bar under Section 16 does not apply to the present case.

21. Section 22 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 empowers the Court to grant relief for possession in a suit for specific performance. The Plaintiff is entitled to possession of the scheduled property upon execution of the Sale Deed.

22. Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 provides for award of compensation in lieu of or in addition to specific performance. The Plaintiff reserves her right to claim compensation for the loss and inconvenience caused due to the Defendant's breach.

23. Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 provides that all agreements are contracts if they are made by free consent of parties competent to contract, for a lawful consideration and with a lawful object. The Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024 is a valid and enforceable contract within the meaning of Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

24. Section 53A of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882 provides protection to a transferee who has taken possession of the property or paid advance consideration. The Plaintiff has paid substantial advance consideration and is entitled to the protection under Section 53A.

25. Article 54 of the Limitation Act, 1963 prescribes a limitation period of three years from the date fixed for performance, or if no date is fixed, when the plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. The present suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

VII. SC

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE PRINCIPAL CIVIL JUDGE (COMMERCIAL DIVISION), BENGALURU

COMMERCIAL ORIGINAL SUIT NO. _____ OF 2024

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A company incorporated under the Companies Act, 2013,
Having its registered office at MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon,
D/o ________, Aged about 40 years, R/o MG Road, Bengaluru,
Karnataka – 560001                                                    ... PLAINTIFF

VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A limited liability partnership registered under the
Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008,
Having its registered office at Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh,
S/o ________, Aged about 48 years, R/o Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Karnataka – 560066                                                    ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT

The plaintiff above named submits this plaint as under:

PART I: PRELIMINARY STATEMENT AND STATEMENT OF TRUTH

1. The plaintiff, TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd, is a company incorporated under the Companies Act, 2013, carrying on business in software development and information technology services, with its registered office at MG Road, Bengaluru. The plaintiff states that the facts set out in this plaint are true to the best of the plaintiff's knowledge and belief, formed on the basis of records and documents available with the plaintiff.

2. The defendant, CloudBridge Systems LLP, is a limited liability partnership registered under the Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008, having its office at Whitefield, Bengaluru, and is represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh. The defendant is a software development company engaged in providing enterprise software solutions.

JURISDICTION

3. This Hon'ble Court has jurisdiction to try this suit as the cause of action arose within the territorial limits of Bengaluru, and the defendant resides and carries on business at Whitefield, Bengaluru, which falls within the jurisdiction of this Court. The subject matter of the suit is a commercial dispute as defined under Section 2(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and the suit value exceeds Rs. 3,00,000/- (Rupees Three Lakhs), being a commercial dispute of specified value.

4. By virtue of Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, this suit may be instituted in a Court within whose local limits the defendant resides or carries on business or works for gain, or where the cause of action wholly or partly arises. The defendant carries on business at Whitefield, Bengaluru, and the cause of action arose at Bengaluru, thereby conferring jurisdiction on this Hon'ble Court.

5. By virtue of Section 9 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, the Civil Court has jurisdiction to try all suits of a civil nature excepting suits of which cognizance is either expressly or impliedly barred. The present suit for recovery of damages for breach of contract is a suit of civil nature and no bar exists for its cognizance by this Court.

FACTS OF THE CASE

6. On 01.02.2024, the plaintiff entered into a Software Development Agreement with the defendant for development of an Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system for a total contract value of Rs.45,00,000/- (Rupees Forty-Five Lakhs only). A true copy of the Software Development Agreement dated 01.02.2024 is filed herewith as ANNEXURE-A.

7. Under the terms of the Agreement, the plaintiff agreed to make milestone payments as deliverables were completed by the defendant. The first milestone payment of Rs.20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only) was to be paid upon execution of the agreement, prior to commencement of development work.

8. On 01.02.2024, the plaintiff paid the first milestone payment of Rs.20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only) to the defendant via NEFT transfer bearing UTR No. ICIC20240201456789. A true copy of the NEFT receipt is filed herewith as ANNEXURE-B.

9. Under the terms of the Agreement, the defendant was obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system by 01.05.2024. The delivery timeline was an essential term of the contract, and the plaintiff relied upon the defendant's commitment to timely delivery for planning its business operations.

10. The defendant failed to deliver the first module of the ERP system by the stipulated deadline of 01.05.2024. The plaintiff did not receive any communication from the defendant regarding delay or extension of time prior to the deadline.

11. On or about 15.06.2024, the defendant abandoned the project and communicated its inability to proceed with the development work. The defendant has failed and neglected to return the advance payment of Rs.20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only) received from the plaintiff. True copies of the email correspondence evidencing abandonment of the project are filed herewith as ANNEXURE-C.

12. The plaintiff has complied with the mandatory requirement of pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The mediation remained unsuccessful, and the plaintiff was constrained to institute the present suit. [Mediation Certificate No. {{MEDIATION_CERT_NO}} dated {{MEDIATION_DATE}}].

13. The plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 to the defendant calling upon the defendant to refund the advance payment of Rs.20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only) and to compensate the plaintiff for losses suffered due to breach of contract. The defendant failed to comply with the legal notice. A true copy of the legal notice dated 01.07.2024 is filed herewith as ANNEXURE-D.

PARTICULARS OF BREACH AND LOSS

14. The particulars of breach and loss suffered by the plaintiff are as under:

(A) BREACH OF CONTRACT:

(i) The defendant breached the Software Development Agreement dated 01.02.2024 by failing to deliver the first module of the ERP system by the stipulated deadline of 01.05.2024, in violation of the delivery timeline clause of the agreement.

(ii) The defendant further breached the contract by abandoning the project on 15.06.2024 without fulfilling its contractual obligations and without returning the advance payment of Rs.20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only) received from the plaintiff.

(iii) The defendant's conduct amounts to breach of its obligation to perform the contract as stipulated under Section 37 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which mandates that the parties to a contract must perform their respective obligations under the contract.

(iv) The defendant's abandonment of the contract amounts to a breach contemplated under Section 39 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, whereby a party refuses to perform or disables himself from performing his promise in its entirety.
(v) The defendant's failure to deliver the first module by 01.05.2024 attracts the consequences under Section 55 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which provides that when a party to a contract fails to perform at the stipulated time, the contract becomes voidable at the option of the party whose consent was obtained by the promise to perform at the stipulated time.

(B) LOSS SUFFERED BY THE PLAINTIFF:

(i) WASTED EXPENDITURE: The plaintiff paid Rs.20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only) as advance payment to the defendant, which stands wasted on account of the defendant's failure to deliver the contracted software module. The said amount remains due and payable by the defendant to the plaintiff.

(ii) CONSEQUENTIAL LOSS: The plaintiff suffered additional loss of Rs.12,00,000/- (Rupees Twelve Lakhs only) on account of delay in its business operations caused by the non-delivery of the ERP system. The plaintiff had committed resources and made business plans based on the expected delivery of the ERP system. The failure of the defendant to deliver resulted in operational disruptions and additional expenditure. A true copy of the loss assessment report prepared by the plaintiff's auditor is filed herewith as ANNEXURE-E.

(iii) TOTAL DAMAGES CLAIMED: The plaintiff claims total dama
